coredata.dc:title: Graphene-like boron nitride modified bismuth phosphate materials for boosting photocatalytic degradation of enrofloxacin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Zhigang
Chen, Xiaoliu
Di, Jun
Liu, Yiling
Yin, Sheng
Xia, Jiexiang
Li, Huaming
coredata.dc:description:
A novel graphene-like BN modified BiPO4 material was prepared for the first time via a simple solvothermal process with the assistance of reactable ionic liquid 1-decyl-3-methylimidazolium dihydrogen phosphate ([Omim]H2PO4). The as-prepared photocatalyst was characterized by XRD, FT-IR, Raman, XPS, TEM, DRS, BET, PL, EIS and ESR to investigate the structure, morphology, optical property, surface area, electrical property and active species. The photocatalytic activities of graphene-like BN/BiPO4 materials were evaluated by the degradation of antibiotic enrofloxacin (ENR) under UV light irradiation and the 1wt% graphene-like BN/BiPO4 displayed the best activity among the BN/BiPO4 composites. The enhanced photocatalytic activity for the removal of enrofloxacin was attributed to higher separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and the generated more O2
− and OH radicals when the BN was modified on BiPO4. Moreover, a probable degradation mechanism was proposed for the improved photocatalytic activity of BN modified BiPO4.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene-like BN
BiPO4
Enrofloxacin
Photocatalytic
UV light

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the as-prepared BiPO4 and BN/BiPO4 materials.
FT-IR spectra of the obtained pure BiPO4 and BN/BiPO4 materials with different BN contents.
Raman spectra of BiPO4 and 1wt% BN/BiPO4 materials.
XPS spectra of the as-prepared pure BiPO4 and 1wt% graphene-like BN/BiPO4 composite. (a) Survey of the sample; (b) Bi 4f; (c) O 1s; (d) P 2p; (e) B 1s and (f) N 1s.
TEM images of the BN and BN/BiPO4 structures. (a) The TEM image of graphene-like BN; (b and c) the TEM image of 1wt% BN/BiPO4; (d) the HRTEM image of 1wt% BN/BiPO4 structure.
(a) The UV–vis absorption spectra and (b) (αEphoton)1/2 vs. Ephoton curves for the obtained pure BiPO4 and BN/BiPO4 composites.
Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and the corresponding pore-size distribution curves (inset) of the (a) BiPO4, (b) 1wt %BN/BiPO4 materials.
(a) Photocatalytic degradation of ENR in the presence of BiPO4, BN/BiPO4 under UV light irradiation. (b) Kinetic fit for the degradation of ENR with the pure BiPO4 and BN/BiPO4 samples.
Photoluminescence spectra of the obtained pure BiPO4 and 1wt% BN/BiPO4 composites.
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) Nyquist plots of the sample electrodes of pure BiPO4 and 1wt% BN/BiPO4 composites.

DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra recorded with (a and b) pure BiPO4 and (c and d) 1wt% BN/BiPO4 samples in (a and c) methanol dispersion (for DMPO-O2
−) and (b and d) aqueous dispersion (for DMPO-
OH) under UV light irradiation.
XPS Valence band spectra of the pure BiPO4 and 1wt% BN/BiPO4 sample.
Schematic diagram illustrating the separation and transfer of photogenerated charges of BN modified BiPO4 materials under UV light irradiation and the possible reaction mechanism.
Pseudo-first-order rate constants for ENR photocatalytic oxidation under different photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Graphene-like boron nitride modified bismuth phosphate materials for boosting photocatalytic degradation of enrofloxacin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene-like BN has been employed to modify the BiPO4 to acquire higher photocatalytic activity for the removal of enrofloxacin.

A novel graphene-like BN modified BiPO4 material was prepared for the first time via a simple solvothermal process with the assistance of reactable ionic liquid 1-decyl-3-methylimidazolium dihydrogen phosphate ([Omim]H2PO4). The as-prepared photocatalyst was characterized by XRD, FT-IR, Raman, XPS, TEM, DRS, BET, PL, EIS and ESR to investigate the structure, morphology, optical property, surface area, electrical property and active species. The photocatalytic activities of graphene-like BN/BiPO4 materials were evaluated by the degradation of antibiotic enrofloxacin (ENR) under UV light irradiation and the 1wt% graphene-like BN/BiPO4 displayed the best activity among the BN/BiPO4 composites. The enhanced photocatalytic activity for the removal of enrofloxacin was attributed to higher separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and the generated more O2
− and OH radicals when the BN was modified on BiPO4. Moreover, a probable degradation mechanism was proposed for the improved photocatalytic activity of BN modified BiPO4.

ce:section.ce:para: A range of medicinal materials exists in the environment and their threats have caused special attention [1]. Among these drugs, antibiotics such as fluoroquinolones (FQs) were under intensive study for being regarded as emerging micro-pollutants [2]. Enrofloxacin (ENR), a kind of FQs, was widely applied in the treatment of various infections for animals because of the broad-spectrum activity of ENR against a large amount of pathogenic gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria by selectively inhibiting bacterial DNA synthesis [3,4]. Microscale of enrofloxacin has been found in animal manure may threaten the environment of soil and water [5]. Although the pollutant could be removed in sewage treatment plants, it still persisted in the residual sewage sludge after anaerobic digestion, and then it was transferred to soil which caused secondary pollutants or regarded as fertilizer for plants, creating a risk for health when the plants were used for human consumption [6–10].

Recent years, two-dimensional graphene-like structure materials have aroused massive research interest thanks to their fascinating properties [11–15]. For the photocatalysis field, some graphene-like structure materials have been employed for displaying outstanding photocatalytic activity or been able to enhance the photocatalytic activity of the other semiconductor materials [16–23]. For example, graphene-like carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheet has been successful prepared by Niu et al. and displayed advanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity [16]. Dong et al. found that new porous graphene-like carbon nitride (C6N9H3) showed excellent ability for NO removal [18]. The fewer layered MoS2 has been employed to enhance the photodegradation efficiency by coupling with semiconductors such as BiOBr [17], Ag3PO4 [19], Ag3VO4 [23], and C3N4 [22]. Xu et al. has successfully prepared the graphene-like Co3S4/Ag2S which displayed higher photocatalytic activities than the pure Co3S4 and Ag2S under visible light irradiation [21]. These results indicated that the graphene-like structure materials may exhibit superior photocatalytic activity when compared with their bulk counterpart or could serve as effective co-catalysts to boost the photocatalytic activity of primary photocatalysts. By insteading the C atoms of the graphite with B and N atoms, the layered boron nitride (BN) can be obtained with remarkable properties [24,25]. Recent researches have found that when the thickness of BN decreased to few layers, the obtained graphene-like BN could also acted as effective co-catalyst for photocatalysis. Several reports of graphene-like BN based material have been prepared such as BN/WO3 [26], BN/Ag3VO4 [27], BN/BiOBr [28] and the results suggested the introduction of graphene-like BN could effectively promote the visible photocatalytic activity for the degradation of pollutants. However, the relevant information regarding the graphene-like BN based photocatalysts has seldom been reported and no information for the improved photocatalytic activity of antibiotic removal has been reported. It is desirable to construct more efficient graphene-like BN based materials for photocatalytic enrofloxacin removal.

Bismuth phosphate (BiPO4), as a highly efficient oxyacid-type photocatalyst, was first reported by Zhu’s group in 2010 owing to its superior activity than P25 (TiO2) at some aspects [29]. For instance, BiPO4 showed 1–2 times higher photocatalytic activity for the removal of organic pollutant and no deactivation was found during the circulation processes [29–32]. Different methods have been utilized to obtain BiPO4 photocatalyst, such as solvothermal reaction [33], microwave synthesis [34], electrospinning method [35], hydrothermal reaction [29,30] and high temperature hydrolysis [36]. Previous literatures synthesized many BiPO4 based composites, compared the photocatalytic activity and discussed their properties. Pan et al. synthesized the C3N4-BiPO4 hybrid photocatalyst to enhance photocatalytic performance towards methylene blue dye degradation [37]. Gawande et al. fabricated graphene@BiPO4 nanocomposite by a simple microwave assisted hydrothermal method to degrade phenol [38]. However, seldom systems have been reported for the antibiotic enrofloxacin removal. Considering the superiority of graphene-like BN and BiPO4, it may further improve the photocatalytic activity by adopting appropriate method to coupling BN with BiPO4. To the best of our knowledge, there has been no report regarding graphene-like BN modified BiPO4 materials or their applications for environment pollutants treatment.

In this study, graphene-like BN modified BiPO4 materials have been prepared for the first time via ionic liquid ([Omim]H2PO4) assisted solvothermal method. A series of characterizations were employed to explore the structure, morphology, surface area and photocatalytic performance. The as-prepared graphene-like BN/BiPO4 materials have been demonstrated to display enhanced UV light photocatalytic activity for the degradation of ENR. The possible mechanism for higher photocatalytic activity of the graphene-like BN/BiPO4 was proposed and the main active species were determined.

The following chemicals used in this study were analytical grade without further purification. Ionic liquid 1-decyl-3-methylimidazolium dihydrogen phosphate ([Omim]H2PO4) was purchased from Shanghai Chengjie Chemical Co. Ltd. Enrofloxacin (ENR), Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and ethanol were selected from Aladdin industrial corporation, Sinopharm, respectively.

The graphene-like BN was synthesized via calcination with a mixture of boric acid and urea in the mole ratio of 1:24. The white solid was emerged by heating the mixture which was dissolved in 40mL distilled at 80°C. Then calcining the above solid at 900°C for 5h under nitrogen atmosphere to obtain the graphene-like BN [28,39].

The detailed preparation procedure of graphene-like BN/BiPO4 powder was as follows. 2mmol [Omim]H2PO4 which contains several massratios (0, 0.5wt%, 1wt%, 2wt%) of graphene-like BN was immersed in 18mL ethanol. 1mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was added into the above solution under ultrasonic and the suspension was obtained after stirring for 30min. The above suspension was then transferred to a 25mL Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 160°C for 24h in oven. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the products were washed with distilled water and ethanol for three times and dried at 60°C overnight.

The crystal structures and phase data for the photocatalysts were determined by X-ray diffractometry (XRD) on a Shimadzu XRD-6000 X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ=0.154Å) at a scan rate of 7° min−1 from 10° to 80°. The structural information for photocatalysts was obtained through Fourier transform spectrophotometer (FT-IR) using KBr as the standard on a Nicolet Model Nexus 470 FT-IR equipment and micro Raman spectrometer regarding a 532nm laser as an excitation source in a backscattering geometry. The materials were analyzed by an ESCALab MKII X-ray photo-electron spectrometer (XPS) and all binding energies were calibrated by referencing the adventitious carbon (C1s=284.6eV). The shape of the prepared samples was analyzed by a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL JEM-2100F). UV–Vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy (DRS) collect the optical diffuse reflectance spectra of samples on a UV-2450 UV–Vis spectrophotometer and BaSO4 was employed as the reference. Surface areas of the samples were measured by using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method based on a TriStar II 3020 Surface Area and Porosity Analyzer with N2 as absorbed gas. The photoluminescence (PL) intensity of the prepared photocatalysts were detected through employing a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrometer. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were taken with a Bruker model ESR JES-FA200 spectrometer and used 5,5-dimethyl-1-oxidation pyrroline (DMPO, Sigma Chemical Co.) in methanol and water to detect superoxide radical and hydroxide radical, respectively.

The photocatalytic activities of the obtained BN/BiPO4 composite products were investigated by degrading of ENR under UV light irradiation with a 250W high pressure Hg lamp. 30mg of the as-synthesized sample was scattered in 100mL of 10mg/L ENR solutions and the suspension was magnetically stirred for 30min in dark to ensure adsorption–desorption equilibrium before the light was switched on. During the photodegradation, 3mL suspension was sampled at 30min intervals and separated by centrifuging at 13,000rpm for 3min to remove the catalyst inside. Then the supernatant was measured at the maximum absorption wavelength of 273nm on a UV–vis spectro-photometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu).

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed on a CHI 660B electrochemical system which was conducted with a Pt wire served as the counter electrode and saturated Ag/AgCl electrode worked as the reference electrode, while the prepared sample covering on the ITO glass employed as the working electrode, respectively. 0.1M KCl solution containing 5mM Fe(CN)6 3−/Fe(CN)6 4− was used in the standard three-electrode system and 500W Xe arc lamp was served as the optical source.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the crystalline structure and phase of the as-prepared BN/BiPO4 materials were determined by the XRD pattern. The results depicted that the diffraction peaks of (011), (−111), (111), (200), (120), (012), (−202), (112), (022), (130), (−131), (212), (−322) and (040) planes at 2θ=19.1°, 21.4°, 25.3°, 27.1°, 29.1°, 31.2°, 34.5°, 36.8°, 38.6°, 41.4°, 42.8°, 46.3°, 52.1° and 58.8° is corresponding to the standard pattern (JCPDS No. 00-015-0767). The XRD pattern of BN/BiPO4 materials observed no obvious diffraction peaks of BN, which may due to the low crystallinity and the low content of BN.

The presence of BN in the BN/BiPO4 materials was determined by FT-IR analysis, and the result was shown in Fig. 2 . The spectra showed that the weak absorption peak observed at 1394cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of B-N in BN [28]. The appearance of bands at about 527, 551cm−1 were due to ν4 stretching vibrations of BiPO4, while the ν3 asymmetric stretching vibrations were found at 957, 1006, 1079cm−1 and ν1 symmetric stretching mode was observed at 926cm−1 may be assigned to the vibrational behavior of PO bonds in PO4 3− ions [30]. No significant change of the vibrational peaks of PO4 3− ions has been found, which can be interpreted as the stable crystal phase of BiPO4 during the solvothermal reaction. The FT-IR spectra proved that BN was successfully combined with BiPO4 and the BN/BiPO4 materials were obtained.

Raman spectra of pure BiPO4 and graphene-like BN/BiPO4 samples in the 40–1200cm−1 region were shown in Fig. 3 . According to the literature [40], the typical vibrational bands located at 95, 130, 168 and 279cm−1 were corresponded to the BiO bands of BiPO4, while the bands observed at 406 and 554cm−1 can be attributed to the bending vibration modes of the PO4 units. The Raman spectra displayed bands at about 1037 and 968cm−1 may be assigned to the ν3 asymmetric and ν1 symmetric stretching modes of PO bonds in the PO4 units, respectively [41]. No obvious peaks of BN were observed in BN/BiPO4 materials, which is consistent with the XRD result.

In order to confirm the elements and valence of the as-prepared composite, the chemical state of 1wt% graphene-like BN/BiPO4 was carefully revealed by the XPS. The survey scan spectrum of the material (Fig. 4 a) showed that all the chemical elements of Bi, O, P, B, and N of BN/BiPO4 composite were observed. As shown from the high resolution of Bi 4f (Fig. 4b), two peaks at about 159.8eV (Bi 4f7/2) and 165.0eV (Bi 4f 5/2) are ascribed to Bi3+. The peaks detected at around 531.0 and 133.2eV are assigned to O 1s (Fig. 4c) and P 2p (Fig. 4d), respectively. The XPS peak of P 2p is ascribed to the P element of PO4 3−. The signals of B 1s (Fig. 4e) and N 1s (Fig. 4f) indicated the existence of BN in BN/BiPO4. The XPS results were agreed with the FT-IR data, further demonstrating that the BN/BiPO4 materials has been prepared.

The morphology and textural property of BN/BiPO4 materials can be seen from scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). SEM image (Fig. S1) showed that BN/BiPO4 composites were consisted of particles with size range of several decades nanometers. As shown in elemental mapping image (Fig. S2), Bi, O, P, B, and N elements could be seen, which testified that the as-prepared samples were BN/BiPO4 composites. The obtained graphene-like BN exhibited a near transparent sheets and silk-like nanostructure, depicting the ultrathin thickness of the pure BN (Fig. 5 a). The TEM images of 1 wt% BN/BiPO4 was displayed in Fig. 5b and c, and it could be seen that graphene-like BN and BiPO4 have been coupled with each other successfully. The corresponding HRTEM image in Fig. 5d further proved that the material obtained was graphene-like BN/BiPO4 composite. The clear lattice fringe of BiPO4 with d-space of 0.466nm was consistent with the (011) lattice plane, while no lattice fringes clearly observed at BN because of the low crystallinity.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the BiPO4 and BN/BiPO4 were displayed in Fig. 6 to explore the effect of BN modification on BiPO4. It was found in Fig. 6a that BiPO4 and BN/BiPO4 samples displayed the optical absorption at wavelength lower than 300nm, indicating the existence of BN in BN/BiPO4 had no impact on the absorption edge of BiPO4. Seen from Fig. 6b, the band gap energy of the pure BiPO4 was calculated to be 4.08eV by using classical Tauc approach [42], which belong to the range of 3.5–4.6eV for BiPO4 materials reported by previous research [32].

Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms were characterized to obtain the specific surface areas of the as-prepared BiPO4 (Fig. 7 a) and BN/BiPO4 (Fig. 7b) composites. The surface areas of BiPO4 and graphene-like BN/BiPO4 (0.5wt%, 1wt%, 2wt%) were measured to be 21.84, 20.22, 22.93 and 23.25m2/g, respectively. In this system, the surface areas of the BN/BiPO4 samples (0wt%, 0.5wt%, 1wt%, 2wt%) are similarly, thus the surface areas may not be the main factor to affect the photocatalytic activity in this system. Seen from the pore size distribution curves, it was easily found that the presence of BN did not impact the pore size and pore volume.

The photocatalytic activities of the pure and BN modified BiPO4 materials were investigated by the degradation of enrofloxacin (ENR) under UV light irradiation. ENR was a kind of antibiotic, which is hard be degraded by the forces of nature, so it is necessary to explore the effective photocatalyst for the degradation of ENR. The photocatalytic activity of the BiPO4 and BN/BiPO4 was evaluated by the degradation of ENR aqueous solution (10mg/L), and the result was shown in Fig. 8 . Modifying a certain amount of BN to BiPO4 could facilitate the photocatalytic activities and the 1wt.% BN/BiPO4 exhibited the best performance. After UV light irradiation for 120min, 74.5% of ENR was photodegraded by BiPO4 (Fig. 8a), however, 0.5wt%, 1wt% and 2wt% BN/BiPO4 could remove about 79.2%, 91.5%, 83.8% of ENR, respectively. As shown in Fig. 8b, kinetic rate constants for the degradation of ENR by the pure BiPO4 and BN/BiPO4 samples were followed pseudo-first-order kinetics plot. From Table 1 , the rate constants for ENR degradation by the pure BiPO4 and BN/BiPO4 (0.5wt%, 1wt%, 2wt%) were 0.0109, 0.0124, 0.0190 and 0.0144min−1, respectively. It can be found that the degradation rate of 1wt% graphene-like BN/BiPO4 for ENR was the highest. When the mass ratio of graphene-like BN was less than 1wt%, increasing the content of graphene-like BN could enhance the photocatalytic activity by transfering the photogenerated charge carriers effectively. However, too much content of BN caused a decrease for the degradation rate of ENR because it would hinder the light harvesting of BiPO4.

The effective separation of photogenerated charge carriers played important role in photocatalysis field. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were employed to study the transfer and separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers. Fig. 9 showed the PL spectra of the pure BiPO4 and BN/BiPO4 materials. The BiPO4 sample displayed the emission peak centered at around 468nm under the excitation wavelength of 360nm. The PL intensity of the BN modified BiPO4 become weaker than that of the pure BiPO4, implying the presence of graphene-like BN in this system could reduce the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

Moreover, electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) measurements were investigated to show the superiority of 1% BN/BiPO4 material over pure BiPO4 in the transfer and separation of charge carriers. It can be seen from Fig. 10 that 1% BN/BiPO4 photocatalyst displayed a smaller impedance arc radius than that of pure BiPO4, indicating the composite had a lower charge transfer resistance and thus the enhanced photocatalytic efficiency may be achieved [43].

The electron spin resonance (ESR) spin-trap technique was carried out to investigate the effect of BN modification for the active species production during the photocatalysis process [44]. Both the characteristic peaks of DMPO-O2
− (Fig. 11 a and c) and DMPO-
OH (Fig. 11b and d) species can be detected, which indicated that the active species in this system were both the O2
− and
OH. As shown in Fig. 11a and c, the intensity of the characteristic signals of DMPO-O2
− for 1wt% graphene-like BN/BiPO4 samples was stronger than that of pure BiPO4 under UV light irradiation, suggesting that more superoxide radical could be generated in BN/BiPO4 system. The similar result was shown in Fig. 11b and d, revealing the modification of graphene-like BN in this system may also promote the generation of hydroxyl radical. Therefore, the increased amount of radicals may account for the enhancement of photocatalytic activity.

On the basis of the mentioned analysis, the improved photocatalytic activity of the graphene-like BN/BiPO4 under UV light irradiation may due to the synergistic effect. When the BiPO4 was under the UV light irradiation, the photogenerated electrons can be excited from the VB to CB and the holes was left on the VB (Fig. 13 ). The VB edge potential of BiPO4 (2.58eV vs NHE) is more positive than the potential of
OH/OH− (2.38eV vs NHE), proving that OH− can be oxidized to
OH [48]. It is well known that the reduction potential of O2/O2
− was −0.046eV vs NHE [49], which was more positive than the CB potential of BiPO4 (−1.50eV vs NHE), suggesting that O2
− can be generated in this system. When the graphene-like BN was modified in BiPO4, more O2
− and
OH were formed on the surface of photocatalyst as discussed in ESR spectra. According to PL and EIS analysis, graphene-like BN can effectively collected the photogenerated carriers and reduce the recombination of electron-hole pairs, thus more O2
− and
OH could be produced. As a result, the photocatalytic activity of graphene-like BN/BiPO4 was improved for the removal of ENR.

The novel graphene-like BN modified BiPO4 materials have been successfully prepared via a facile solvothermal method. BiPO4 was dispersed on the surface of the graphene-like BN and the obtained photocatalyst exhibited higher photocatalytic activity than the pure BiPO4 for the degradation of antibiotic ENR. Different mass ratios of graphene-like BN modified BiPO4 have been investigated and 1wt% BN/BiPO4 demonstrated the best photocatalytic decomposition ability, and it could remove about 91.5% of ENR under UV light irradiation for 2h. This improved performance was attributed to the increased efficiency of charge transfer, suppressed photogenerated electron-hole recombination and produced more active species after the introduction of graphene-like BN. The development of BN/BiPO4 materials with high photocatalytic activity may provide a promising future for industrial applications to deal with the organic pollutants from sewage water.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21476098, 21471069 and 21576123), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Grants No. BK2012717) and the Doctoral Innovation Fund of Jiangsu Province (KYZZ16_0340).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2016.12.050.
